title: Musicians’ heirs hit out at AI deepfakes as 'digital grave robbing’ - Music Ally
text:
The debates about how AI technology should be regulated continue, with the latest views coming from a group of organisations and heirs representing musicians who are no longer alive – Bill Withers, Cass Elliott, David Rose and Alex North included.
Gathered by veteran attorney Chris Castle and the Artist Rights Institute, they have made a joint filing to the White House’s consultation on AI regulation, which has been fielding the views of
tech giants
and
music-industry bodies
alike.
You can read the filing from the heirs here
: it’s a good primer on some of the AI issues that are currently in sharp focus for the estates of artists and songwriters who are no longer with us. Deepfakes in particular.
“AI fakes can depress licensing rates by flooding markets with inauthentic sound-alikes, undermining price discipline, provenance, and consumers’ trust online,” claims the filing.
“This is a special case of deepfakes: synthetic ‘performances’ engineered to sound like people who have passed away and then distributed without authorization alongside authentic works. Such misattribution misleads the public, distorts history, and erodes the integrity of carefully curated legacies built over decades.”
The filing cites recent events surrounding OpenAI’s release of its Sora 2 GenAI video model and app, and the wave of clips featuring the likenesses of dead public figures that ensued.
“These AI-generated synthetic imitations involuntarily resurrect iconic music performers, sports figures and actors without consent, context, or compensation—reducing cultural heritage to algorithmic spectacle and gratuitous ridicule,” it blasts.
“Users are shown synthetic performances by celebrities long deceased that are created and monetized by corporations with no legal or moral relationship to the underlying works or families. This is not innovation; it is digital grave robbing.”
The filing is less about calling for new laws than it is urging the White House to apply existing laws and regulations to synthetic recordings and likenesses, and clarifying the guidance from federal agencies about how they work.
“Congress and the Administration have powerful tools at their disposal — from reinforcing state police powers and strengthening cooperation with state attorneys general, to clarifying that model weights embedding copyrighted works constitute derivative works and making clear that pirated training data receives no national security exemption,” it concludes.
“They can reject a failed DMCA-style model that burdens artists with enforcement costs and instead assign responsibility to those who create, deploy, distribute, and profit from these technologies… A free society cannot thrive in a hall of mirrors that forgets who is looking into it. Our laws must protect reality itself – its authors, its witnesses, and its inheritors.”
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
